data.title: Paramount Skydance offered $30/share for Warner Bros. Disovery - report
data.description:
• Paramount Skydance (PSKY) offered $30/ share for all of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), according to a CNBC report, which cited people familiar with the matter.  Shares of Warner Bros. rose 4.8% in premarket trading. 
  • The offer would be above the $27.75 a share cash and stock offer that Warner Bros. (WBD) agreed to take from Netflix (NFLX) earlier on Friday. 
  • Netflix (NFLX) earlier said it will acquire Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), including its film and television studios, HBO Max and HBO, for a total enterprise value of ~$82.7 billion.

data.title: Warner’s journey from AOL to AT&T to Netflix marks another chapter in decades of deals
data.description:
Netflix (NFLX) has announced plans to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) for an enterprise value of about $82.7 billion, adding the Warner Bros. film and television studios, HBO, and HBO Max to its portfolio.

The agreement marks the latest turn in a company that has been passed between corporate owners for more than 50 years. 

Warner’s modern ownership changes began in 1969, when Kinney National purchased Warner Bros.–Seven Arts and later rebranded the business as Warner Communications.

In 1990, Warner Communications merged with Time Inc., forming the first iteration of Time Warner and combining film, cable networks, magazines, and music under one banner.

Another milestone came in 1996, when Time Warner acquired Turner Broadcasting, adding CNN, TNT, TBS, Cartoon Network, Hanna-Barbera, and Turner’s classic film library.

The company underwent its most famous deal in 2001, when AOL acquired Time Warner in a then-record-setting merger valued at $182 billion. By 2003, the AOL name was removed as the dot-com bust reshaped the company. Throughout the 2000s, Time Warner streamlined itself—spinning off Warner Music Group in 2004, separating Time Warner Cable and AOL in 2009, and divesting Time Inc. in 2014.

AT&T (T) entered the picture in 2018, purchasing Time Warner for $85 billion and rebranding it as WarnerMedia. That structure lasted until 2022, when AT&T spun off WarnerMedia and merged it with Discovery to create Warner Bros. Discovery. 

Now, it's Netflix's turn to take control as Warner begins yet another chapter—its fourth major transition in half a century—as the streaming giant aims to reshape its long-term content strategy.

data.title: Docusign in focus as analysts cut price targets despite 'solid' results
data.description:
Docusign (DOCU) shares fell 8% in premarket trading on Friday as a couple of Wall Street firms cut their price targets on the electronic signature company, despite reporting what some called “solid” third-quarter results and guidance.

“DOCU reported its FY3Q26 results featuring beats across the board as the company saw strong demand for IAM and eSignature, but the performance will be overshadowed by the relatively conservative outlook,” Wedbush Securities analysts wrote in a note to clients. 

“While the company produced a relatively solid quarter, we believe the raised outlook remains conservative,” the analysts added. They kept their Neutral rating on the stock, but lowered the price target to $75 from $85.

Piper Sandler also lowered its price targets on Docusign, moving to $75 from $90 after the results.

For the period ending Oct. 31, Docusign said it earned an adjusted $1.01 per share as revenue rose 8.4% year-over-year to $818.35M. Included in that was $801M from subscriptions and $17.4M from professional services and other revenue. Analysts had expected the company to earn an adjusted $0.92 per share on $807.1M in revenue.

Looking ahead to the fourth-quarter, Docusign expects sales to be between $825M and $829M, with the $827M midpoint slightly below the $827.4M forecast. Subscription revenue is expected to be between $808M and $812M, while billings are forecast to be between $992M and $1B. Adjusted gross margin for the period is likely to fall within a range of 80.8% to 81.1%.

Docusign also upped its forecast for the rest of the fiscal year, as it now sees revenue between $3.208B and $3.212B, above a prior range of $3.19B to $3.2B. Subscription revenue is now expected to be between $3.14B and $3.144B, compared to a prior range of $3.12B to $3.13B.

data.title: Canada unemployment rate unexpectedly drops
data.description:
Employment rose by 54,000 (+0.3%) in November, fueled by an increase in part-time positions. The employment rate edged up 0.1 percentage points to 60.9%, far surpassing the consensus expectation of a 5,000 decline.

Employment marked the third straight monthly gain. Combined job growth across September, October, and November (+181,000; +0.9%) helped offset a sluggish start to the year, when employment saw little net movement from January through August.

Private sector employment increased by 52,000 (+0.4%) in November, while employment in the public sector and among the self-employed showed little movement.

The unemployment rate dropped by 0.4 percentage points to 6.5%, beating expectations for a higher 7% reading. The employment rate ticked for the third consecutive month. On a year-over-year basis, however, the employment rate remained unchanged.

data.title: Domo CEO reduces duties; shares down
data.description:
• Domo (DOMO) chief executive officer Joshua G. James will reduce his duties effective December 4, 2025.
  • James will remain chief executive officer despite scaling back responsibilities.
  • Chief technical officer Daren Thayne has been appointed interim principal executive officer, effective December 4, 2025.
  • DOMO shares down 17% premarket on Friday.

data.title: Parsons plunges as Peraton wins multi-billion dollar air traffic control contract
data.description:
Parsons (PSN) -18.3% pre-market Friday after the Federal Aviation Administration awarded a multi-billion dollar contract to overhaul the aging U.S. air traffic control system to Peraton, a national security company owned by Veritas Capital.

The U.S. Congress has provided the FAA with $12.5 billion as an initial down payment to carry out the project, but Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy said in July that the current cost estimate of the overhaul totaled $31.5 billion.

The Trump administration considers the project a following widespread tech outages at several airports this year.

Peraton will be the single integrator to manage the massive project and deliver it on time without air traffic disruptions, the FAA said.

A new FAA fact sheet on the project shows the agency will seek 44 airports outfitted with new replacement surface radars, 200 airports with surface awareness initiative surveillance technology, 89 airports with new terminal fight data manager tools, and the first new consolidated air route traffic control center since the 1960s.

Raymond James downgraded Parsons (PSN) two notches to Market Perform from Strong Buy, as analyst Brian Gesuale said the decision "will be a major surprise to investors, dents the psyche of the stock, and eliminates significant organic upside to numbers in 2026-27."

William Blair analyst Louie DiPalma said "many investors considered Parsons the favorite to win" the contract, and lowered his FY 2026 revenue estimate to $6.51 billion from $6.71 billion and adjusted EBITDA estimate to $631 million from $643 million.

data.title: ChargePoint achieves positive revenue growth and narrows Q3 loss on subscription momentum
data.description:
After several uneven quarters with decelerating revenue, ChargePoint (CHPT) reported a solid third quarter, narrowing its loss and achieving 6% revenue growth to a better-than-expected $106.7M.

“ChargePoint is showing early signs of stabilization,” said Needham analyst Chris Pierce in response to Q3 results. “Delivering a beat with in-line guidance, continued subscription momentum, improving cost discipline, and a meaningfully strengthened balance sheet,” following its debt exchange with Eaton.

Revenue growth for the quarter was fueled by a 15% increase in subscription revenue and 7% gain in networked charging systems revenue.

Profitability improved as well with ChargePoint (CHPT) reporting an unadjusted loss of $2.23 per share versus a loss of $3.56 a year ago, although 7 cents below expectations. Additionally, on the increase in subscription revenue and subscription margins, adjusted gross margin improved 700 basis points to a record high of 33%.

The company also issued Q4 revenue guidance that was better-than-expected with a range of $100M to $110M with a midpoint of $105M that is above the $102.9M estimate.

“ChargePoint’s expanding partnerships (namely Eaton) and new platform launch positions the company to capture the [European EV adoption] momentum,” Pierce said, adding that “bulls can argue that the worst of the reset is behind the company, setting the stage for CHPT to re-emerge as leverage returns to even modest demand recovery.”

Agrees Roth’s Craig Irwin. “The debt refinancing was a clear positive, and now ChargePoint needs to take more aggressive action targeting adjusted EBITDA breakeven.”

On the company’s earnings call with analysts, CFO Mansi Khetani acknowledged the focus on positive EBITDA and noted that “EBITDA gains will come with growth in revenue which we are significantly focused on. With the new products and the increased demand in Europe and the Eaton partnership, we think the second half [of the year] should be pretty strong.”

ChargePoint (CHPT) shares lurched higher in Thursday’s after hours trading and are now up 10% ahead of Friday’s open.

data.title: ClearBridge Energy Midstream Opportunity Fund announces balance sheet information
data.description:
• ClearBridge Energy Midstream Opportunity Fund (EMO) announced on Friday the unaudited statement of assets and liabilities, the net asset value and asset coverage ratio of the fund as of November 30, 2025.
  • As of November 30, 2025, the fund's net assets were $978.4M, and its net asset value per share was $48.88. 
  • The fund’s asset coverage ratio regarding senior indebtedness was 791%, and the fund's asset coverage ratio with respect to total leverage was 545%.
